articles.title: South Sudan risks return to full-blown civil war as violence escalates
articles.content:
At least 169 killed in raid near Sudan border as clashes between government and opposition forces intensify

South Sudan is reeling from an escalating conflict between the government-aligned army and opposition forces and allied groups that observers say risks returning the country to a full-blown civil war.

Violent confrontations in the world’s youngest country between the military, which is loyal to President Salva Kiir, and insurgents believed to be allied to the suspended vice-president, Riek Machar, have increased in recent weeks.

Continue reading...

articles.title: ‘Imperialist undertones’: global south condemns US-Israeli war with Iran
articles.content:
China calls it unacceptable to ‘kill leader of sovereign state’, while South Africa questions ‘pre-emptive’ justification

  • Middle East crisis – live updates

The US-Israeli war on Iran has been condemned as illegal across much of the global south, with China saying it was unacceptable to “blatantly kill the leader of a sovereign state”.

Many countries objected that negotiations between the US and Iran over its nuclear programme and missile capability were not given a chance to succeed before Washington and Israel began bombing, and analysts often saw the war in terms of a colonial-style exercise of might.

Continue reading...

articles.title: Iran Live Updates: Israel Begins ‘Broad Wave of Strikes’ on Iran’s Infrastructure
articles.content: The U.S. announced the names of four soldiers who were killed by an Iranian drone on Sunday. President Trump said airstrikes had killed potential new leaders of Iran, leaving it unclear who would run the country.

articles.title: Israel Seizes Chance to Crush Hezbollah and Remake Mideast Amid Iran War
articles.content: With the war against Iran underway, and the U.S. military as a powerful ally, the Israeli government is seizing its chance to move against other adversaries.

articles.content: Chancellor Friedrich Merz’s visit to Washington, long planned, is now likely to be dominated by discussions of the attack, which Europeans did not take part in.

articles.content: Disregarded by President Trump over Iran, Europe’s leaders are adapting to a world in which they are little more than bystanders.

articles.content: Cross-border fighting between Afghanistan and Pakistan has entered its fifth day. The UN says the violence has killed 42 civilians and forced around 20,000 families to leave their homes.

articles.title: Trump, Tehran and the long shadow of Washington's checkered history of regime change operations
articles.content: Various US presidents have sent military forces to remove unpopular rulers abroad. Their track record is mixed. Initially, Trump claimed that regime-change was a goal in the current Iran war, too.

articles.title: Who is Ali Larijani, the unofficial strongman in Iran?
articles.content: With US-Israeli airstrikes killing Khamenei and other Iranian leaders, veteran politician Ali Larijani seems to be calling the shots. Larijani comes from a powerful family — but was never fully embraced by hardliners.

articles.title: German customs net ketamine cache in golden garden gnomes
articles.content: X-ray anomalies helped German customs officials crack a daring ketamine smuggling operation. A bumper haul of the drug was found stuffed inside a troop of fancily decorated outdoor figurines at Cologne/Bonn Airport.

articles.title: Nepal elections: Gen Z hopes for genuine political reforms
articles.content: Nepal is gearing up for parliamentary elections, months after a youth-led uprising forced out the previous government.

articles.title: Iran attacks on Gulf oil and gas sites trigger energy fears
articles.content: Iranian attacks on regional refineries and gas facilities, and oil tankers are increasing global energy supply fears, risking major economic fallouts from Asia to Europe, and leaving consumers bracing for higher prices.

articles.title: Britain halts student visas for Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar and Sudan
articles.content: Britain will stop issuing education visas to nationals from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar and Sudan, and work visas for Afghans, the government said on Tuesday, as it moves to curb asylum claims via legal routes. The Home Office said asylum applications from students of those countries rose more than 470% between 2021 and 2025.

articles.title: Ray Takeyh: 'Iran is not a personalised dictatorship, it's an ideological system'
articles.content: Carys Garland is pleased to welcome Ray Takeyh, Senior Fellow for Middle East Studies at the Council on Foreign Relations (CFR). As war rages in Iran, Mr. Takeyh warns that America's "war of choice" is guided by an improvised military strategy that swings wildly between grand ambitions of regime change and more narrowly framed goals, including disabling Iran’s missile program, neutralising its proxy networks, and permanently ending its nuclear aspirations. All are open-ended commitments and lack a clearly articulated exit strategy.

articles.title: Unpopularity of Iran war 'almost unprecedented' this early on, expert says
articles.content: Speaking with FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney, Stephen Zunes, Professor of Politics and Programme Director for Middle Eastern Studies at the University of San Francisco, says that "even in the most controversial wars, like Iraq, when the war initiates, people tend to rally around the flag [...] this is remarkable that at the very beginning of the war there's overwhelming opposition not only from liberals and the left, but much of Trump's spaces as well".

articles.title: What we know about the deadly strike on a primary school in Iran
articles.content: Iran held a mass funeral this Tuesday for at least 165 schoolgirls killed Saturday in a strike on Minab. But online, some users claim the tragedy was staged by Iranian authorities. In this edition of Truth or Fake, we confirm the strike hit the Shajareh Tayyebeh girls’ school, a building separated from nearby IRGC military barracks for nearly a decade. We also examine claims blaming U.S.-Israeli strikes for the attack, as well as allegations of a failed Iranian rocket launch.

articles.title: Can Iran's economy survive if the war drags on?
articles.content: Iran took the risky gambit of closing the Strait of Hormuz, which could backfire and hurt its own economy.  France 24's Yuka Royer speaks with Iranian-Austrian economist Mahdi Ghodsi from the Vienna Institute for International Economic Studies about Iran's unsustainable economic path and whether it's headed for hyperinflation.

articles.title: African leaders call for dialogue amid escalating Iran crisis
articles.content: In tonight's edition, the Global South widely condemns the war in Iran, with many countries accusing the US and Israel of violating international law and acting unilaterally. And the United States imposes sanctions on the Rwandan army and several top military officials over their role in fighting in the eastern Democratic ​Republic of Congo.

articles.title: Trump threatens to cut off trade with Spain over Iran war refusal
articles.content: Trump threatened Tuesday to sever all trade with Spain after it refused to let US planes use its bases to attack Iran. Spain's left-wing government led by Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez, one of Europe's most defiant leaders against Trump, has said that bases long used by US forces could only be allowed for activities consistent with the United Nations Charter. FRANCE 24's Sarah Morris reports from Madrid

articles.title: US has 'seemingly used diplomacy as a fig leaf to prepare for military strikes', expert says
articles.content: Speaking with FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney, Simon Mabon, Professor of International Politics and Middle East Studies at Lancaster University, says that the prospect of Iran coming back to the negotiating table is very remote, arguing that the United States has lost any goodwill because during the past two rounds of diplomatic talks "diplomacy has seemingly been used as a fig leaf to prepare for military strikes".

articles.title: 'We're going to unleash Chiang on these people': Rubio says US stepping up Iran campaign
articles.content: The US Secretary of State Marco Rubio says that the United States is stepping up its campaign in Iran, widening the scope of airstrikes in the country. FRANCE 24's Fraser Jackson reports from Washington.

articles.title: 'Not Churchill': Trump criticises UK PM Starmer over Iran strikes
articles.content: US President Donald Trump on Tuesday criticised UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer for not initially permitting American warplanes to use UK bases to strike Iran, saying "this is not Winston Churchill that we're dealing with".

articles.title: Trump says 'everything's been knocked out' in Iran but offers no clear plan for war

articles.title: Trump lashes out at Spain, says he is 'going to cut off all trade'
articles.content: President Donald Trump said he wants to “cut off all trade with Spain” over NATO spending, adding “we don't want anything to do with Spain." His comments came during a meeting between him and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz.

articles.content: The death of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has plunged Iran into a wartime leadership crisis. A temporary council runs the country while the Assembly of Experts — whose headquarters were hit in US-Israeli strikes on Tuesday — must select a successor.

articles.title: Goldman’s Solomon Surprised by ‘Benign’ Markets on War

articles.title: Oil Jumps Again as US Escort Plan for Hormuz Fails to Bring Calm
articles.content: Oil extended gains as fresh attacks flared in the Middle East and traders weighed a US plan to insure and escort tankers passing through the Strait of Hormuz, with traffic in the vital waterway all but halted.

articles.title: Asian Investors Repositioning Portfolios, JPMorgan's Craig Says
articles.content: Kerry Craig of JPMorgan Asset Management says Asian markets are experiencing continued repositioning by investors. He sees the moves as driven by rotation within portfolios rather than thinking about "a big flight away from risk assets at the moment." (Source: Bloomberg)

articles.title: Nidec Shares Rise as Investors Bet on Rebound From Scandal
articles.content: Nidec Corp.’s shares surged after an investigation into a long-running financial scandal found systemic issues with its accounting but not its manufacturing operations, giving investors hope the business can still be salvaged.

articles.title: Aramco Explores Oil Exports From Red Sea to Avoid Hormuz
articles.content: Saudi Aramco is exploring the option of delivering more cargoes to Yanbu, a port on the Red Sea that’s situated outside the Persian Gulf, where dozens of ships are hunkered down as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed.
